name: Close tokio-port latency gap (target: get rtt_p50 from 36µs back toward main's 21µs)

description: Baseline tokio-port (current state). Main branch baseline ~21µs p50; gap = ~15µs.

asi.hot_path_hops: stdin_task -> stdout (client) and PTY -> stdout, each routed through mpsc + reader/writer split
asi.first_optimization_idea: fold server-side reader into direct PTY writes (skip control_tx hop for Input messages)

description: Server reader writes input directly to PTY (skip main task hop). Arc<AsyncFd> + Mutex for write serialization.

asi.hypothesis: skipping main-task hop on inbound (client->PTY) saves one mpsc + one task wakeup per byte
asi.next_idea: skip outbound (PTY->client) hop similarly: main writes directly to OwnedWriteHalf when single live client, or have writer task do nonblocking try_write before yielding

description: Replace writer_task with direct writes from main; replay-only task. Backfires.

asi.hypothesis: skip writer_task hop by having main write directly to client write_half via Mutex
asi.lesson: writer_task lets multiple chunks batch through a single drain loop; main writing directly forces a writable().await per chunk, which is more expensive than the task hop. The mpsc + writer_task design absorbs bursts.
asi.next_action_hint: go back to writer_task; instead try to reduce the writer_task's overhead — e.g. unbounded mpsc, or have writer use try_write before writable().await, or merge multiple queued packets into one write
asi.rollback_reason: throughput regressed 42% (37 -> 21 MiB/s); RTT also worse (20.6 -> 25.4)

description: Switch outbound mpsc bounded→unbounded so send() is sync.

asi.hypothesis: switch outbound mpsc from bounded(64) to unbounded so main's send is sync (not .await)
asi.lesson: bounded mpsc's send.await provides a critical yield point; without it, main task keeps producing without giving runtime a chance to schedule writer_task. Removes natural cooperative scheduling.
asi.next_action_hint: leave queue bounded; investigate other paths. Try: instrument writer_task to coalesce multiple packets into one write_all (one syscall instead of N).

name: Close tokio-port throughput gap without regressing rtt_p50

description: Baseline: tokio already +15% ahead of main on throughput, tied on latency. The 'throughput gap' was a measurement artifact.

asi.finding: no throughput gap exists under fair measurement conditions; tokio is +15% AHEAD on throughput and tied on latency
asi.hypothesis: establish throughput baseline; assess whether the gap is real
asi.previous_session_artifact: loaded system (load avg 6.7, 62% sys CPU) skewed the comparison to make tokio look behind

description: Writer batches queued packets via try_recv into one write_all. Backfires.

asi.hypothesis: writer_task batches queued packets via try_recv into one write_all syscall to reduce per-packet syscall overhead
asi.lesson: batching with cap-64 mpsc means main fills the queue, blocks on send().await, writer drains all 64 in one big write_all (~256KB). The big write blocks waiting for kernel socket buffer space, while many small writes succeed individually. Batching backfires when the batch exceeds socket SNDBUF.
asi.next_idea: tight PTY drain loop (loop try_io until WouldBlock) — different optimization, doesn't bunch up writes. Or: cap the batch size so it stays under SNDBUF.
asi.rollback_reason: throughput regressed 15%; latency also slightly worse

description: Tight PTY drain loop (try_io until WouldBlock) — no measurable improvement on macOS.

asi.hypothesis: tight PTY drain loop (try_io until WouldBlock) cuts wakeups under sustained output
asi.lesson: macOS PTY scheduler delivers ~1024 bytes per wakeup AND deschedules between reads; consumer almost always finds WouldBlock on the second try_io. Optimization is sound but produces no measurable win on this platform. Could help on Linux (no PTY chunking) but we shouldn't keep changes that don't measurably help here — it adds branching to the hot path for no gain.
asi.next_idea: Look elsewhere. Possible: reduce per-packet allocations (Packet::clone() in fan-out, Vec for payload). Or: profile to see where time actually goes.
asi.rollback_reason: thr 33.75 vs baseline 34.9 (-3%, within noise but no improvement); rtt unchanged
